focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 13

The landing reserved for honored guests had been scrubbed until it smelled expensive. Someone up on Gilt Crescent had dumped soap, lemon peel, and brass polish over the usual breath of warm mud and algae mash, and the whole waterfront wore the effort badly. Under it all the Sallow still stank of sweet rot. The launches rocked against their ropes with garlands of cut glass and paper pennants, and every pennant carried the same pale green letters: CELADON CROWN. The words were printed so many times they looked less like language than wallpaper for a rich man's delusion.

Silas stood there and felt his collar drinking him. Gideon's tailor had sent over a cream summer coat that fit across the shoulders and nowhere else, and the cloth held heat like spite. His right wrist, still not right since the trough, ached inside the cuff whenever he flexed his hand. Below the rail the quay churned with ferry crews, shop girls, buyers from the ridge, newsboys with bundles under their arms, and the sort of men who came to any public arrangement in hopes that free liquor might fall out of it. Brass from the municipal band skimmed over the water. Somewhere a barge choir was testing the first lines of the regatta hymn. Even the gulls sounded instructed.

He ought to have laughed. A month ago he would have laughed until his ribs hurt. Gideon had turned a living civic crisis into a floating advertisement and dressed it up like a bride from a warehouse catalogue. But laughter needed looseness, and Silas had come tight with purpose. They had sat half the night at Junie's table dividing mouths and places, and he'd slept too little to loosen what the planning left knotted in him. He had a folded scrap in his inner pocket with three names and three uses in Inez's hand, not because h...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ed at the edges of color, refusing to settle into a single obedient glow.

Silas thought of Gideon's basin under glass, every skiff arranged, every route bundle tied, every poster already dried to the board. He thought of Vero's patched tin, warm through his fingertips. One room tried to make a city behave. The other had kept a city alive without ever pretending it belonged to one hand. For the first time since this whole mess had turned his mouth into civic weather, he could see the shape of the fight clearly: one market mouth trying to swallow every useful one.

"We'll need more mouths than ours," he said.

"We've got them," she said. "We only need them saying what works before his cards reach supper."

"That'll be noisy."

"Good."

The word settled between them with more force than anything louder could have managed.

Below the walk the drainage cut carried old runoff south toward the river. The water took scraps of light from every window it passed and broke them up on the surface. One house threw green onto it. Another laid blue across it. Farther down, some child had insisted on a name at supper and left a bruised pink thread in the current. The colors never held for long. The water kept breaking them apart.

Inez set her hand on the rail beside his, a plain offer of shared balance. Shared weight was enough. "Then that's the work," she said. "Keeping it in use so nobody gets to own it alone."

He looked at their hands on the wet wood, her scar silver in the low light, his stained fingers, the city breathing around them through a hundred uncoordinated mouths. Under the deeper theft, under the heat and shame and coming spectacle, purpose finally took a shape he did not need to sell.

Tonight the word would go house to house. By tomorrow it would need Junie's tables.
